speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6, interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You stated earlier that you were in Bradford until about 1942. Was that about right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that period, period of time, you had constant communication with your parents, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sorry, yes. Continuous, but the frequency changed radically at the outbreak of war. In other words, between-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- between getting to Bradford in March '39 and the outbreak of war in September '39, it was very frequent correspondence. Between September '39 and '42, it was-- the only communications that were allowed in wartime were Red Cross messages, which were messages not exceeding-- personal messages not exceeding 25 words, which were sent by the sender to the Red Cross in Switzerland, and passed by the-- by the Swiss Red Cross to the other-- to the recipient. And then the answer came back the same way. It took a long time. And so, therefore, it was limited already by, by that fact-- the frequency was limited by that fact; and, of course, also by the need to be extremely circumspect; and worst of all, to be-- to say everything [LAUGHS] in 25 words.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have kept a few of those-- of those messages to this day. And it's amazing what one can pack into 25 words. But-- so the, the, the nature and frequency of the communications changed very radically at the outbreak of war. When was the last communication you had with, with your parents and with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42-- it would be in late '42, early '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reason being that my sister was arrested in July of '43 and deported to the east to a concentration camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a week after her [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] birthday. Which birthday-- in '43, 21st birthday. And my parents shortly after that-- all this I found out much later-- made-- took steps to-- took the first steps in making the arrangements to go-- to try and go over the border illegally, leave Germany illegally. Because the legal ways had been exhausted, and there was just no way out for them. And they knew that they would be deported sooner or later-- rather sooner than later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- they were taken up with other tasks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and concerns. And I couldn't say now whether the last message-- clearly, it did not get, get to me. Any letter wasn't written any later than the time that they left, sometime in '43-- that they left the house and went into hiding, which they did for a time before trying to-- trying the border crossing. Did you then eventually learn what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my sister had been deported, they, they figured that, that there was no longer any reason for them to, to stay around. There wasn't my sister to protect. There wasn't-- the only thing that they could do is to try and get out at all costs, including illegally. And it was a big risk. But they had to take it, they felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left their house and moved into the apartment of a close family friend-- non-Jewish. She already had been a friend ever since we moved to Berlin, and-- or even before. And this was a lady who was the wife of the architect Mies van der Rohe-- the one that was rather famous and built the buildings in Chicago. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ada, A-D-A. And the surname was a-- sounds more like a Dutch name. But they were German. Mies, M-I-E-S; van, V-A-N; der, D-E-R; Rohe, R-O-H-E. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he got these commissions-- her husband got these commissions in America in the early '30s or mid-30s, I believe. And then his, his name became, became very well-known here and had a very good, lucrative practice. And they, they were separated, basically. He stayed here. And she stayed there because the girls-- they had three daughters who were in school. And she couldn't just up and go. And besides, there were other considerations as well. They were very anti-Nazi. And if there are any good Germans-- which I have many times in my life doubted-- these would certainly belong to them, and she particularly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She hid my parents, at, of course, obvious cost and risk to her life and liberty and family for several months, it seems, and-- several weeks at least. I don't know the exact time. And from there, they had arranged certain stops before they got to the border. They must have had false papers. I don't know the, the details of that. But they got to the border all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What border?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had these different stops. The border was near Innsbruck-- Swiss border, border between Germany, or rather, Austria-- which was part of Germany at that time-- and, and Switzerland in an area called the Vorarlberg. It's the, the Swiss name for it. V-O-R-A-R-L-B-E-R-G, which is not far from Innsbruck, which is a major [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Austrian town in the-- in the Alps. not in the Alps, near, near the Alps, very hilly country. And from there, they, they, they-- from the last stop, they tried to cross the border there, were caught by a patrol, which apparently mainly consisted of a young recruit, who was still, still very raw and still had his-- had some humanity left, and accepted a bribe and let them go. Then they tried again. And this time, they were caught for real by a real patrol, real soldiers, accompanied by real SS. And, and that was it. They were at first, it seems, put into a concentration camp near Innsbruck and from there, sent to Auschwitz. I have had no-- and no one has had any news from them. And the assumption, of course, is that they perished there. They were killed. And I didn't know for many years that it was Auschwitz because the information that I got, of course, was from the people that helped her-- helped them-- helped my parents escape. They didn't know the details of what happened after they were caught, since only somebody close to the Nazis or close to the government would, would be able to find that out. And they weren't that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found this out when I-- on a visit much later after the war, in '71, my first visit to Berlin after the war. I, I went to the Office of Jewish Affairs, which had been set up and which collected documents and various other things-- information.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they handed me a large envelope with the passports of my parents and my sister, and a Gestapo report headed-- Gestapo, Gestapo letterhead-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in very thorough-- typically German thorough format, telling of the arrest of the-- of the married couple leaving, who were trying to escape from Germany and blah, blah, blah, and were sent, were arrested-- and placed under arrest and sent to Auschwitz. And that's the first word I heard-- the first time I heard the word 'Auschwitz' in, in the-- in this whole matter. Because, as I say, nobody-- it came [LAUGHS] indeed from-- the government source for anything is the Gestapo. Nothing has been heard from them. Nothing has been heard from my sister or about my sister or about them. My-- the only thing I know about my sister's fate is that she was apparently sent to Riga in Latvia, which had a whole history of ghettoization and then ghetto extermination and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as the receiving locale for deported German Jews and Austrian Jews, and-- from various sources, mainly German, though, mainly from Berlin, it seems. Whether she got there via Poland, where she might have-- she might have spent some time in a camp, in Lublin or not, it doesn't matter. She ended up in Riga. And there has been no word. I have spent a great many years and a great many efforts in trying to trace her, particularly, because she is the-- was the most likely to have-- to have maybe survived in some form or other. And I was interested to find out that there is an association in America of survivors of the Riga Ghetto, enough of them to actually form an association. I've been in touch with them. I've advertised. I've put notice into, into their newsletter. I've been sent a copy, a, a, a photo, in fact, of someone that may have known her, which, of course-- but when I saw the photo, I realized that she wasn't any of the four girls that were on the-- on the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever learn the circumstances of her arrest or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --her deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were-- they may be of some interest-- the-- which, to illustrate the limits on the freedom of movement of Jews in Berlin at that time, and I'm sure in other places in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews were allowed-- were not allowed to use public transportation, except on those routes which they had specific and written permission for on their person, which they had to show. I have such a permit for my father, which was in the effects that I just described, which shows me the format. And it gives him permission to go-- to make multiple journeys. It doesn't say per day. But it's renewable annually. So it seems as if the number of trips was not-- was not limited, not on this paper anyway, between his residence, which was on the outskirts of, of Berlin and the inner city of Berlin. So that's all I know. My sister, apparently-- now I understood, once I had that paper, I understood better what happened to my sister, which I had until then, not quite understood because I didn't know the nature of this document. She was working-- it was forced labor-- in a place somewhere in a Berlin suburb. I think it was a Siemens. I was told it was a Siemens subsidiary, the big electric company Siemens, which still exists. And she was-- she had a permit, same as my father. Hers said she is authorized to go from her residence to her place of work and back on a certain metro line-- the yellow line, let's say. She was at the station. She was meeting some friends who were also from work, who were also either going to the office who would come with her from the office. She was talking to them. And they were kind of playing around, and, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of very lightheartedly before they went, went their ways, or before going into the-- into the office. And one of them-- anyway, to cut a long story short-- kind of playfully pushed her, without meaning to, over the general space of the station. She found herself just one step-- she couldn't catch herself before she actually put a step-- a foot-- a step inside the track that was-- that belonged to the-- not the track, the, the, the part of the station that belonged to the red line, let's say. Immediately, an SS man, who was standing there, put his hand on her shoulder, asked to see her permit. He said, you are not authorized to be here and arrested her. And took her home-- she was allowed to pack a suitcase. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he took her away. Or they took away. By this time, he might have had back-up. I don't know. And that was the last anybody saw her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you gain this information?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] I got this information from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ada-- Ms. Van der Rohe-- who must have been told it by my parents, who must have been told it by my sister when she came home briefly to pack her bag before she left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before she was deported, after she'd been arrested. A very brief space of time-- I mean, my parents must have been absolutely horrified. They said, how come? How come? And I'm sure she told them the whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have told Ada because that's the only way that she could have known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to 1971, when you learned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the-- of Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in connection with your parents, what did you believe after the war happened to your parents? What was your thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: My rational thinking told me that, that they were dead, that they had-- they were not in good health. And all the things that one learned-- and I was, remember, I was in intelligence-- US intelligence at the end of the war, just after the war. And I had access to certain-- to certain information. And all of that later became general information. And all that went learned about the divisions that the Nazis made among the people that were deported shows that the, the only people that really had a chance were those that were young able-bodied, et cetera. My father was, at that time, in '43, he would be 58 years old. He had never been an athletic-type person. 58 in 1943 in Germany is not like 58 [LAUGHS] in, in, in, in the US today. My mother, of course, was ailing on a fairly long-term basis-- this, this asthma and other related things. So she was not well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- there's, there's no reason, rational reason to, to believe. The only thing is that there is-- that hope springs eternal. But hope gets extinguished as the mortality-- even if they did live and escape-- it gets extinguished as one gets older and as one's parents would be older.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there came a time when I had to simply say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I tried, tried, tried, tried-- I, I kept trying to find out more from all-- god, there's a myriad of organizations, lists. One can always find out more information. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in regard to my sister, I still to this day haven't reached the point-- even though the chances are so minimal as to be zero-- one cannot say that beyond all doubt, she was only two years older than I-- would be only two years older than I-- that she is dead. I cannot say that. My parents, the same is true. But, on the other hand, my father would be 111 years [LAUGHS] at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother would be-- no, I mean, you know, this, this is not, not reasonable-- and, and to have escaped and to have survived. And they had my address. They knew my address-- my addresses after the war. They knew other people's address. They had my address. They were not out of-- if they were around, if they were alive, I would have heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you experience any grieving at all for your parents throughout this entire time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult to [SIGHS] sort of experience a, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a discrete [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grieving period when you don't know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when everything I just said-- the situation is, is as I just said. Grieving for the likelihood, yes. But it isn't an absolute grieving. It isn't as absolute. It becomes absolute as the years go by and, and you're unsuccessful in your efforts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It takes longer, obviously-- because she is much younger-- with my sister. And as I say, it's not quite terminated yet. But-- and I have, in fact, every, every intention to, to, at some point, go to Riga after the opening up of, of that part of the world and to make inquiries. But I don't imagine that there would be any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you briefly summarize your steps from Yorkshire to date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I'll try and be brief about that. In London, I was-- I got a job, as I think I mentioned. I was, very shortly after that, called up for war work. I should add that I made efforts during the entire-- these entire years to get into the armed forces. But my nationality, which was still German-- even though I was classified as a refugee from Nazi oppression, a bona fide refugee-- and why I was not treated-- i.e. in turn, I was not treated like, like real Germans-- Nazi-type Germans. And nonetheless, I was not accepted into the army. I made efforts to get into the US Army as soon as-- as soon as the-- as soon as the US entered the war. And in fact, I now remember-- I hadn't told this before-- is that I even made-- when Mexico-- I read somewhere that Mexico entered the war. And I wrote to the Mexican embassy. I got a letter back in Spanish, and I-- which, which I knew by that time. I had taken Spanish classes at one of my-- one of my-- one of my years in Bradford. And, and the, the answer was a very polite no. In fact, I don't think any, any Mexican forces were engaged in the-- in the-- in the war. But it was a declaration. It was a political thing. Anyway, I couldn't get in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people who could not-- were not in the armed forces-- that were able-bodied, and in a certain, certain age group were called up for war work. It was war work. It was either work on the land, where you did harvesting and, and muckraking and all the things that agricultural laborers do. And-- or it was factory work. Factories needed-- munitions factories and other factories-- war-important factories needed workers. So I figured that at least on the land I would be in open air. And I chose that part. So I was working on the land for the next three years or so, until early '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-between, I managed to-- I had-- I should add that I had managed to matriculate from school, get-- in other words, high school matriculation in England was the same thing as a high school certificate and university entry examen combined. And I had passed that before leaving Bradford. I had come back from the evacuated school because they, they, they were hopeless. They couldn't teach me anything. And I was-- the level was too low under those circumstances-- and went to evening classes, had a job in the meantime, and took the-- took the matriculation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as soon as I could, I was going to enter university. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I took more courses to prepare me for that while I was on the land, did a lot of reading, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, an opportunity presented itself to join a corps that the US Intelligence was putting together, which was called the Disarmament Division, part of the US Army Air Force. And my cousin by marriage-- the brother of my, my cousin, who was also a refugee about my age-- a little older-- in fact, he's about five years older-- he was also in England. He married the daughter of a Jewish couple there that-- from Germany that had also landed there. So he was married. Her brother had gone to America and was now in the army and was back in England in uniform. And he told me about this. And I immediately enlisted his help, and-- as far as he could. And I made my application. And I got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was at last, I was going to be in-- at my post in, in Germany a little before the armistice. But the armistice happened a few days too early. [LAUGHS] And I only got there after the armistice-- middle of May, a few days after the-- armistice was, I think, the 8th of May of, of '45. So I was with Uncle Sam-- a very interesting time, but I won't go into detail about that-- rooting out-- helping to root out Nazis, and more particularly, Nazi arms caches that the-- that the Nazis had stashed away; and also investigating war, well, war industries that had collaborated very strongly with the Nazis and had used large, large quantities of slave labor-- foreign slave labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was there until VJ Day. VJ Day-- we-- the, the, the corps was-- the division I was in was suspended. And their work was done. And I guess the attention then shifted to, to Japan. Any case, I was back in England. I managed to get into university in '46. I took-- I managed to get a scholarship the year after that. Until then, I was working in the university library and going through the first year, and then got my degree in economics in '49 from the University of London, having studied at the London School of Economics. It's part of the university. And then had a job in the north of England for a few years. Met an American girl on holiday in Paris, where I was also on holiday. And she came over to Manchester where I was living-- Manchester, England, and got married. We got married. And she then wanted to go back. She had no intention of staying in England. And when she saw what the rations-- food rations for visiting foreigners were-- one egg a week was as an example of the-- of the kind of shortages that we had. I mean, I had been-- I had been there the whole war. So I, I was used to it. But she was not. And she had an apartment in New York. So as soon as I could get a visa, which was guaranteed by my uncle-- my mother's brother, who had gone to America in '37-- I came over to New York. And I've lived, apart from a couple of foreign assignments, I have lived in America ever since and had various jobs, which I don't think are too relevant here. I took a graduate degree in-- at Columbia in economics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got married. We were divorced. I had one children from that first marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The son from that first marriage, who is now 36, is Alexander-- Alexander Walter, Walter after my father. I then got married again. I lived, by this time, in California, working at Stanford Research Institute. And I married again. And I have one son by that second marriage. That ended in divorce sometime later. And then I wasn't married for quite a while. And I'm now-- then I also landed at the World Bank, which has been my last career job, apart from consultant work that I keep doing. And I was with the World Bank for about 10 years plus and just left them on the regular-- on my regular job there about three years ago. But I've done consulting work for them quite frequently since then. And I was-- I met my present wife. And we were married. And we were together for quite a while. And we were married in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Leslie. She is a doctor. And her name is Leslie Ford. She kept her former name. And-- one gets tired of changing names all the time. Besides, if one has a-- once one has a professional identity, it's rather hard to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the end of tape number 6.
